frameworks.name: Feedback Loop Mapping
frameworks.description: Every persistent problem exists inside a system with feedback loops that reinforce it. Mapping loops before intervening prevents solving the symptom while the system regenerates the problem.
steps: Identify the core variable you want to change (revenue, energy, output quality, etc.)
Map what causes that variable to increase and decrease — these are your drivers
For each driver, ask: does the result of changing this variable feed back to affect the driver itself?
Classify loops: Reinforcing (R) — change amplifies itself. Balancing (B) — change triggers correction back toward equilibrium.
Identify which reinforcing loops you want to accelerate and which balancing loops are suppressing growth
Intervene at the loop level, not the symptom level — address what maintains the pattern

examples.situation: Consulting revenue stuck despite more proposals sent
examples.loop_map: More proposals → more rejections → less confidence → weaker proposals → more rejections (reinforcing negative loop). Root: proposal quality, not volume.
examples.intervention: Break the confidence loop — fix one proposal component at a time, get early feedback before full submission.

examples.situation: Productivity crashes in the afternoon every day
examples.loop_map: High morning output → skipped lunch → energy crash → poor afternoon work → overcompensating next morning → skipped lunch again (reinforcing degradation loop).
examples.intervention: Protect the recovery input (lunch, movement) to interrupt the loop — not 'work harder in the afternoon.'

antipatterns: Treating symptoms without mapping the loop — problem returns
Adding more force to a balancing loop — system pushes back harder
Missing the reinforcing loop that's driving decay — confusing a structural problem for a motivation problem

frameworks.name: Leverage Point Identification
frameworks.description: Donella Meadows identified that systems have intervention points of vastly different effectiveness. Most people intervene at the lowest-leverage points. Highest leverage comes from changing the rules, goals, and paradigms of a system.
steps: List all interventions currently being attempted on the problem
Map each to Meadows' leverage hierarchy (low to high): Numbers → Buffers → Flows → Feedback loops → Information → Rules → Goals → Paradigms
Identify the highest leverage point you have access to
Design intervention at that level — not the most obvious level
Test with minimum viable change: can you shift the rule or goal without full system redesign?
Monitor for unintended consequences — high-leverage interventions have large second-order effects

leverage_hierarchy.name: Flows (rates)
leverage_hierarchy.leverage: Medium — changes speed but not structure

leverage_hierarchy.name: Information (who gets what)
leverage_hierarchy.leverage: High — people cannot act on information they don't have

leverage_hierarchy.name: Rules (incentives, constraints)
leverage_hierarchy.example: Changing pricing model, contract terms, hiring criteria

leverage_hierarchy.name: Goals (what system optimizes for)
leverage_hierarchy.leverage: Very high — everything else serves the goal

leverage_hierarchy.name: Paradigms (mental models)
leverage_hierarchy.example: Changing what you believe about yourself, the market, or what's possible
leverage_hierarchy.leverage: Highest — paradigm determines what goals are even conceivable

antipatterns: Spending all effort at level 1-3 while ignoring level 6-8 — working harder inside a broken system
Changing a high-leverage point without anticipating second-order effects
Assuming the stated goal is the real goal — systems optimize for what is actually measured, not what is declared

frameworks.name: Unintended Consequences Mapping
frameworks.description: Every intervention in a system produces effects beyond its intent. Second-order thinking maps these before action, not after.
steps: State the intended effect of your proposed intervention
Ask: what else changes when this changes? List 3-5 second-order effects
For each second-order effect, ask again: what does this cause? (third-order)
Identify which effects are positive (preserve/amplify) and which are negative (mitigate/monitor)
Check for delayed effects — systems often respond slowly, creating the illusion that an intervention has no effect before a large effect materializes
Check for opposite effects — some interventions cause the problem to worsen before improving (J-curve), or trigger system resistance that cancels the intervention

examples.intervention: Hire more salespeople to increase revenue
examples.second_order: Sales compensation costs increase. Onboarding consumes existing team's time. Cash burn accelerates before revenue materializes.
examples.third_order: If revenue doesn't materialize fast enough, cash position becomes critical. Existing team morale affected by onboarding burden.
examples.lesson: The intervention is correct — but timing and cash position must be pre-validated before committing.

examples.intervention: Work longer hours to increase output
examples.second_order: Fatigue accumulates. Quality of work degrades. Recovery time increases.
examples.third_order: Net output over 4 weeks may be lower than sustainable pace. Burnout creates extended recovery period that erases gains.
examples.lesson: The correct leverage point is output per hour (systems, delegation, elimination), not hours.

antipatterns: Confusing first-order effect (intended) with system outcome (net effect of all orders)
Ignoring time delays — intervention appears to fail, is doubled down on, then massively overshoots
Missing compensating feedback — system generates resistance that partially or fully cancels intervention

frameworks.name: Bottleneck Analysis (Theory of Constraints)
frameworks.description: Goldratt's core insight: every system has exactly one constraint limiting total throughput. Improving anything other than the bottleneck does not improve system output. Find it. Fix it. Repeat.
steps: Map the full flow of the system: inputs → process steps → output
Identify where work accumulates (backlog) or where flow slows — that is the current bottleneck
Subordinate everything else to the bottleneck: do not optimize upstream steps that will only feed a jam, do not build inventory the bottleneck cannot process
Exploit the bottleneck: maximize its utilization before adding capacity. Every minute of bottleneck downtime is throughput lost forever.
Elevate if necessary: add capacity to the bottleneck only after full exploitation
Once resolved, identify the new bottleneck — it will shift

examples.situation: Agency delivering client work: proposals written fast, discovery calls booked fast, but projects consistently delayed
examples.analysis: Bottleneck is delivery capacity, not sales. More sales without delivery capacity creates client dissatisfaction and cancellations.
examples.intervention: Freeze sales growth temporarily. Fix delivery: systemize delivery, hire delivery talent, reduce scope creep. Then re-open sales.

examples.situation: Creator: content ideas abundant, but posting rate is 1x/week despite wanting 4x
examples.analysis: Bottleneck is editing/production, not ideation. Generating more ideas does nothing.
examples.intervention: Batch-record raw content in one session. Hire editor or simplify production standard. Bottleneck shifts from production to distribution.

antipatterns: Optimizing non-bottleneck steps — improves efficiency with no impact on throughput
Adding capacity everywhere rather than at the constraint — expensive, ineffective
Confusing busy-ness with throughput — high utilization of non-bottleneck steps may indicate the bottleneck is starving

frameworks.name: Stock and Flow Modeling
frameworks.description: All systems can be modeled as stocks (accumulated quantities) and flows (rates of change). Modeling the stocks and flows of a system before intervening reveals why it behaves the way it does.
steps: Identify the key stocks in your system (things that accumulate over time): cash, skills, reputation, relationships, energy, knowledge, inventory
Identify the inflows (what increases the stock) and outflows (what depletes it)
For each stock, determine: Is the inflow rate higher or lower than the outflow? Is this the intended state?
Identify which stocks are critical (if this stock hits zero, the system fails): cash runway, energy, key relationships
Protect critical stocks first — build buffers before optimizing flows
Identify which stock increases produce the most leverage: compounding stocks (skills, reputation, relationships) have exponential return on accumulation

stock_types.examples: Skills, reputation, relationships, content library, code base
stock_types.strategy: Prioritize — each unit compounds on previous units
Protect and replenish — once depleted to zero, system collapses
Scale with demand — premature scaling is waste; late scaling is bottleneck

antipatterns: Optimizing flows while ignoring critical stock levels — fast flow into an empty tank doesn't help
Treating compounding stocks as interchangeable with non-compounding — reputation and cash are not equivalent stores of value
Ignoring slow-to-build stocks (reputation, relationships) until urgently needed — cannot be created on demand

prompts.name: Feedback Loop Map
prompts.template: Map the feedback loops maintaining this problem: [describe the persistent problem]. What reinforcing loops are keeping it in place? What balancing loops are preventing improvement? Where is the actual leverage point for intervention?

prompts.name: Leverage Point Audit
prompts.template: I'm trying to change [outcome]. My current interventions are: [list]. Map each to Meadows' leverage hierarchy (numbers → flows → feedback → information → rules → goals → paradigms). Identify the highest-leverage point I'm currently ignoring and what intervention at that level would look like.

prompts.name: Second-Order Consequences Map
prompts.template: I'm planning to [proposed action]. Walk me through second and third-order consequences. What else changes when this changes? What system responses should I anticipate? What are the delayed effects? Any compensating feedback that could cancel the intervention?

prompts.name: Bottleneck Diagnosis
prompts.template: Diagnose the bottleneck in my system. The system: [describe full flow from input to output]. Where work accumulates: [describe]. My current optimization focus: [describe]. Identify whether my optimization target is the actual constraint, and what full exploitation of the real bottleneck looks like.

prompts.name: Stock and Flow Audit
prompts.template: Audit the stocks and flows in my system: [describe your business, career, or project]. Identify: (1) critical stocks that must not hit zero, (2) compounding stocks I should be investing in but may be neglecting, (3) flows that are miscalibrated (inflow too low, outflow too high). Prioritize interventions.

prompts.name: Full System Diagnosis
prompts.template: Run a full systems diagnosis on: [describe the situation — persistent problem, stuck outcome, or system you want to redesign]. Cover: feedback loops maintaining the current state, leverage points available to me, second-order effects to anticipate, bottleneck if applicable, and critical stocks to protect. Prioritize the single highest-leverage intervention.

examples.title: Agency Growth Plateau
examples.context: Agency at $40K/month for 6 months. Owner working 60+ hours. Adding salespeople, running more ads. No growth.
examples.analysis: Bottleneck is owner as delivery bottleneck, not sales. Every new client increases owner hours. Reinforcing loop: more clients → more owner hours → lower quality delivery → churn → need more clients → more ads.
examples.intervention: Freeze new client acquisition. Document delivery system. Hire delivery lead. Owner exits bottleneck. Then re-open sales.
examples.leverage_point: Level 6 (Rules): change the rule from 'owner delivers' to 'system delivers.' Not Level 3 (more sales flow).
examples.outcome: Agency scaled to $120K/month in 8 months with owner at 35 hours.

examples.title: Skill Development Plateau
examples.context: Consuming 10+ hours of learning content per week. Skill level not improving. More courses, same result.
examples.framework_applied: Leverage Point Identification + Stock and Flow Modeling
examples.analysis: Inflow (learning input) is high. Outflow (practice, application) is near zero. The stock of skill doesn't accumulate from consumption alone — it accumulates from deliberate practice with feedback. The bottleneck is application, not input.
examples.intervention: Cut consumption by 80%. Replace with deliberate practice in the skill area with feedback loops. 2 hours of practice beats 10 hours of consumption.
examples.leverage_point: Level 4 (Feedback loops): add the feedback mechanism that was absent (practice + correction). Not Level 3 (more input flow).

vocabulary.definition: Feedback loop where change amplifies itself in the same direction. Can drive exponential growth or exponential collapse. The engine of compounding — positive or negative.
Feedback loop that opposes change and drives a system back toward equilibrium. The source of system resistance to intervention.
An accumulated quantity in a system — the current level of cash, skills, reputation, energy, inventory. Can only be changed by flows over time.
The rate at which a stock increases (inflow) or decreases (outflow). You can change flows immediately; changing stocks takes time.
The single step in a system limiting total throughput. Improving anything other than the bottleneck does not increase total output.
A place in a system where a small intervention can produce large changes in system behavior. Meadows identified 8 levels from lowest (parameters) to highest (paradigms).
The consequence of a consequence. The effect that results from what your first-order intervention causes. Often more significant than the intended first-order effect.
The lag between an action and its visible effect in a system. Time delays cause oscillation, undershoot, and overshoot — and cause people to abandon interventions before they work.
In Theory of Constraints: the rate at which a system generates its goal output. Only the bottleneck limits throughput.
System response that opposes and partially cancels an intervention. The harder you push, the harder the system pushes back.
The shared set of assumptions underlying a system's goals and structure. The highest-leverage intervention point because paradigm determines what goals are even imaginable.
Goldratt's principle: adjust all non-bottleneck activities to serve and support maximum throughput of the bottleneck. Counterintuitive — make non-bottlenecks less efficient to optimize the system.

sources.title: Thinking in Systems
sources.relevance: Foundational: stocks, flows, feedback loops, leverage points

sources.title: The Goal
sources.relevance: Theory of Constraints — bottleneck identification and exploitation

sources.title: The Fifth Discipline
sources.relevance: Systems archetypes, learning organizations, feedback structure

sources.title: An Introduction to General Systems Thinking
Second-Order Thinking
sources.relevance: Practical second-order thinking in decisions and markets

v78_consciousnesses: House v2.1 (differential diagnosis — bottleneck and root cause identification)
Ancient One v3.0 (temporal — stock accumulation and flow rates over 1/5/10-year horizons)
Meta-2.5 Systems Thinking (primary alignment — this cartridge operationalizes Meta-2.5)
Constraint Mapper (hard vs soft constraints in system structure)
Deadpool v2.2 (challenge: is this a systems problem or a simpler problem being overcomplicated?)
master_laws: Law 13 (Second-Order — this cartridge is the full implementation of Law 13)
Law 11 (Leverage — Meadows hierarchy is a leverage point taxonomy)
Law 19 (Compound Interest — reinforcing loops and compounding stocks are the mechanism of compounding)
Law 28 (Systems — replicable systems require understanding the system structure)
sovereign_needs_served: Order (primary — understanding system structure produces clarity over confusion)
Truth (systems diagnosis reveals actual root cause vs surface symptom)

decision_engine_integration.bottleneck_analysis: Feeds qBestMoves — highest-ROI intervention is typically bottleneck removal
decision_engine_integration.feedback_loop_data: Pattern_Recognition_Engine (A.8) surfaces empirical feedback loops from historical data
decision_engine_integration.stock_monitoring: Finance_Insights (D1) tracks financial stocks (cash, runway) as live system variables

4_gate_relevance.gate_2_true: Systems diagnosis must reflect actual system structure — Rogan + Axiom Guard applies to causal claims
4_gate_relevance.gate_3_leverage: Leverage point identification is the implementation of Gate 3 — this cartridge operationalizes the gate
4_gate_relevance.gate_4_aligned: Intervention must serve the 10-Year Horizon — high-leverage system changes can compound positively or negatively over time

4_gate_validation.safe: PASS — no sensitive domain; no professional consultation caveat required
4_gate_validation.true: PASS — Meadows hierarchy and Goldratt TOC are well-validated frameworks; examples are structural (not empirical claims)
4_gate_validation.high_leverage: PASS — systems thinking is paradigm-level intervention (Level 8 Meadows); highest-leverage framework in Order cluster
4_gate_validation.aligned: PASS — directly serves Order need; Laws 13, 11, 19, 28 compliance; feeds DECISION_ENGINE D20 and Pattern_Recognition_Engine
